plan.description: This Data Management Plan describes how research data generated in the project will be created, documented, stored, shared, and preserved. The project will generate interdisciplinary research data related to the development and evaluation of biomaterial scaffolds for osteochondral tissue regeneration, including scaffold fabrication, characterization, biological data, and supporting documentation.
plan.title: Development of metabolite-delivering osteochondral scaffolds for osteochondral tissue regeneration using cold sintering methods (1.1.1.9/LZP/2/25/261)

systemField.label: Description
systemField.description: Briefly describe the context and purpose of the DMP

systemField.label: Language
systemField.description: Select the language of your DMP

referenceField.label: Researchers
referenceField.description: Add here the names of people that have produced, processed, analysed the data described in the DMP.

referenceField.label: Organizations
referenceField.description: Add here the names of the organizations contributing to the creation and revision of the DMPs

referenceField.label: Funding organizations
referenceField.description: Select a funder of your research or add new

referenceField.label: Grants
referenceField.description: Find the grant of your research or add new

referenceField.label: Project
referenceField.description: Projects in Argos are perceived as distinct activities falling under a grant or common activities under different grants in collaborative schemas, eg open call for contributions. Please complete it for the grant associated to your organization if your project falls under this category. In all other cases, please leave blank and it will be autocompleted.

systemField.label: Access Rights
systemField.description: Choose how the DMP is displayed after is published on Zenodo. By choosing Open Access, the DMP will be open on Zenodo after the Publication Date. By choosing Restricted Access, the DMP will be restricted after the publication is made.

referenceField.label: License
referenceField.description: Assign a license to your DMP by selecting the most appropriate from the list.

reference.label: Development of metabolite-delivering osteochondral scaffolds for osteochondral tissue regeneration using cold sintering methods (1.1.1.9/LZP/2/25/261)

description.description: The dataset will be generated through the development and evaluation of osteochondral scaffolds within the project. It will capture data related to scaffold fabrication parameters, physicochemical and mechanical characterization, degradation and release behaviour, and in vitro biological evaluation, including biocompatibility and cell–material interaction.
description.label: Dataset on osteochondral scaffold fabrication, characterization, and biological evaluation

descriptionTemplate.label: AQUASERV | generic | template
descriptionTemplate.description: A generic data set description template for the AQUASERV project

fieldSet.title: Data Types
fieldSet.description:
What types of data will you create or re-use in your project. What are their formats?

Examples
  • I will be collecting images (jpg), sound files (mp3) and videos (mp4)
  • I will be collecting DNA sequences (FASTQ) and creating bioinformatics outputs (various text files)
  • I will be collecting tabular measurements from my aquaculture experiment in a spreadsheet (CSV)

fieldSet.title: Data Creation
fieldSet.description:
How will/were the data types/data sets described in 1.1.1. created?

Examples
  • The data will be the result of field collections
  • The data will be the output of the following: a FlowCam, Fluorescence microscopy,  etc...
  • The data will be the collected measurements made from my aquaculture experiments

fieldSet.title: Published Data Re-use
fieldSet.description:
Will you re-use any previously published data? 
If Yes: state why you are using the data, and where the data originate from

Examples
  • I will include occurrences and species range data from EMODnet to complement my study of biodiversity of the Baltic Sea in 2025
  • I will use existing DNA sequence data from EMBL ENA in my molecular phylogenetic analysis

fieldSet.title: Data size
fieldSet.description: What is the expected size(s) (in GBs) of the data set(s) that you intend to create and/or re-use? If you are unsure, make a guess.

section.description: What types of data will be created or re-used in your TA project?
section.title: Types of data and usage

fieldSet.title: Data Publication Repository
fieldSet.description:
In which repository, database, or data catalogue will you publish your data set(s)? Please indicate a publication venue for each of your data types or data sets, if you will publish in more than one place. 

Recommedations:

If you are doing organismal experimentation, use the following services/repositories:

  • BioStudies - for study metadata and result data in tabular format, with some scope to include images (depending on size). All organismal studies should include a BioStudies accession to record project metadata, and possibly to deposit result data
  •  BioSamples - to register organismal samples in the BioStudies accession and add sample specific metadata 
  •  EMBL ENA - for sequence data, incl. metagenomics, transciptomics, etc
  • ArrayExpress - microarray or qPCR data
  • ChEMBL - molecular assay results, structures, etc…
  • Metabolights - for spectrographic data (LC-MS, UV-vis, etc)
  • Protocols.IO - for protocols if these are an output
  • EurOBIS - for biodiversity data
  • Ecotaxa - for biodiversity imaging
  • BioImage Archive - for other types of biological image
  • Seanoe -  for other types of marine-derived data (not better placed above)
  • Pangaea - everything else Earth Science related, but not marine (very broad)
If you are collecting only environmental/physical data (that is not organismal sample metadata), consider using:
  • SeaDataNet
  • Seanoe
Only if there is not a more data-specific repository you could use should you use Zenodo or Figshare, or some other (unstructured) repository - this is likely to be a very rare occurance. If in doubt, please contact the AQUASERV Helpdesk

You may be required to archive your data in a repository of your university/organisation. If that repository is not accessible to everyone, then you should also publish your data in one of these other mentioned repositories. But even if that is open to everyone, you should publish in one of these other mentioned repositories as your data are more globally findable in those.

fieldSet.title: Metadata collection
fieldSet.description:
How will you collect your dataset description and provenance metadata? 

Reminder: metadata are the descriptive data that contextualise and describe the actual data themselves and make your data findable, re-usable, and reproducible. This includes information that describe these aspects:
  • WHO - did the work
  • WHEN - was the work done
  • WHERE - was the work conducted, the field work/sampling done
  • WHAT - parameters and samples (use BioSamples accessions if appropriate) did you collect/work with
  • WHAT - instruments, software, platforms wer used
  • WHAT - what data files are in your data set and what do they contain 
  • HOW - were the sampling, sample processing, experiments, and software analysis done (register methods and protocols with protocols.io)
If you create a BioStudies accession for your project, then your data can be included in that accession and linked to any other data you publish in other repositories.

Examples
WHO, WHEN, and WHERE
I will collect the following information in a spreadsheet: the names and ORCID IDs of all the people involved in the work, the coordinates of my sampling field work, the name of service provider where I did the work, the dates of the sample collection and the subsequent sample and data processing.
I will include these information in the metadata form when I publish my data on BioStudies. 

WHAT
I will ask the TA service provider for the types/names and IDs of all the instruments and devices I will use.
My samples will be given an ID that I will create, and these IDs will be included in my experimental data and be linked to all photographs I take of the specimens. 
My samples will also be documented with the WoRMS species names. 
I will record the name and version of the software that I used (it will be my own software that I will subsequentely make available via GitHub).
I will create a README to describe each file in my data set. 
I will include these information in the metadata form when I publish my data on BioStudies.

HOW
I will also ask the TA service provider for the URL/DOI of their protocols that I followed and will record this in my README. If those protocols are not published, I will instead ask for a PDF copy and I will include this as part of my dataset. 
I will publish my own protocol on the protocols.io AQUASERV Community. I will then add all the DOI to my README.
I will only use my own software, and the settings will be included within the code I will publish. 
I will include these information in the metadata form when I publish my data on BioStudies.

section.description: How you will make your data and metadata findable (N.B. metadata are the data that describe and contextualise the actual data that you are collecting in your TA project, and make them understandable to everyone else.)
section.title: Findable

section.description: How the data will be made accessible
section.title: Accessible

fieldSet.title: Open Access Data Repositories
fieldSet.description:
Are the repositories you indicated in 2.1.1 for publication of data and metadata Open Access (i.e. can anyone see the data and metadata, or is access limited to certain people)?

Your answer should be YES. If not explain, why.

fieldSet.title: Persistent URLs and idenifiers
fieldSet.description:
Do the repositories you indicated in 2.1.1 ensure that the data is assigned a long-term URL (html link that takes you to the metadata or the data) or a DOI?

Your answer should be YES. If not, explain why.

fieldSet.title: Licence
fieldSet.description:
What open licence will you publish your data under? 

Your answer should be CC-BY 4.0. If not, explain why.

fieldSet.title: Data Publication Embargo
fieldSet.description:
Will you apply an data publication embargo?

As described in the terms agreed upon when your TA project was accepted, all data created by your study should be uploaded to the appropriate repository as soon as possible after its collection. This is to ensure that data are deposited when they are most memorable and are not lost to time - unfortunately a great deal of data that are collected are never published. After data deposition to a repository, an embargo on its public release from the repository can be enacted. 

If YES: For each of your data sets, indicate if an embargo will be applied to give time to publish your science or seek protection of the intellectual property (e.g. patents), and specify why and how for long the embargo will apply. Explain who will remove the embargo when it is over.

fieldSet.title: Restricted Data Usage
fieldSet.description:
Are there are restrictions on re-use of the data you will create?  For example, due to Intellectual Property rights, sensitive data (esp. if human data). 

If YES: how will access be provided to the data, both during and after the end of the project?

Example 
  • I am collecting data about ecologically sensitive species, and so will keep some locality information (specific locations for example) private

fieldSet.title: Unpublished Data
fieldSet.description:
Are there any data you create that you cannot, or will not, publish publicly?

Note that null results and incidental data should be published - i.e. negative results and/or data that is not useful to your science (hence will not be published in scientific journal) should nevertheless be published in an Open Access repository.

Your answer should be NO. 
if YES: please explain why

fieldSet.title: Open Data Formats
fieldSet.description:
Are the formats in which you publish your data open?

If NO: please list the non-open formats and explain why you have to use them.

A closed format needs a proprietary software application to be read (i.e. typical examples are spreadsheets in MS Excel format xlsx. Some instruments (e.g. LC-MS) also produce data that can only be read by proprietary (commercial) instrument-specific software - these data should be converted to open formats before publication. 
An open format can be opened and read by anyone without needing specific software to do that. Examples are: CSV, TSV, FASTA, FASTQ, PDF, JPG
If in doubt: try wikipedia! 

NB some formats are not open but are openable with free software: for example, MP4 and XLSX. If you can convert these to open formats (e.g. CSV instead of XLSX), please do so.  

Examples
  • The instruments I will use at the TA station produce data in proprietary formats: I will discuss with the TA station how I can convert these to open formats before publication.
  • I will convert my MS Excel files to CSV before I publish them, so they are in an open format

fieldSet.title: Vocabularies and Ontologies
fieldSet.description:
Will you use controlled vocabularies or ontologies in your data and metadata?

Controlled vocabularies contain terms that are agreed by scientific communities working within the field of expertise. These terms are written in a controlled way and have one definition that everyone has agreed on. The terms can be found on the web via a URL (web address), and will have an ID by which they can be referred to as well as the actual term name (a string). 
Ontologies are glorified vocabularies, as they additionally ascribe meaning and relations between terms of a vocabulary. 

Examples are:
  • MIXS - Minimum Information about any (x) Sequence (MIxS) standard
  • ENVO - The Environment Ontology
  • Marine Regions - A standard list of marine georeferenced place names
  • EDAM - Ontology of bioscientific data analysis and data management
  • EFO - Experimental Factor Ontology
  • WoRMS -  World Register of Marine Species
  • NCBI Taxonomy Database - Classification and nomenclature for all organisms

Examples
  • I will use terms from ENVO to describe the habitats relevant to my experiment. I will use their recommended URLs for the terms   (http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/ENVO_12345678)
  • I will use NVS to describe measured parameters and their units. I will use their recommended URLs for the terms  (http://vocab.nerc.ac.uk/collection/P01/current/ABCDEDG/)
  • I will use MiXS when uploading my data to ENA, as this is mandatory
  • I will use NCBI taxonomy for all taxon names, and will use their recommended IDs for the names (NCBI:txid123456)

section.description: How will you make the data you publish interoperable with other data (i.e. how will you standardise your data)?
section.title: Interoperable

fieldSet.title: Methods and Protocols
fieldSet.description:
Will all your methods, protocols, operating procedures, computer scripts, software execution parameters (etc) be published  as Open Access?

If YES: add an explanation of where and how. 
If NO: please explain why

Example
  • I will publish my methods in protocols.io and my analyses scripts and software will be stored on GitHub and published via Zenodo.

section.description: How will the data publication enhance data re-use
section.title: Re-useable (and reproducible)

fieldSet.title: Now send an email to aquaservhelpdesk@ualg.pt
fieldSet.description:
Before submitting and finalising your DMP to Zenodo, please inform the AQUASERV Helpdesk. We will review the DMP and provide recommendations as necessary.

This DMP is an evolving document which is intended to be updated as the project progresses; when you update your DMP in Argos, please inform us directly.

Thank you: AQUASERV WP3 Open Science and Data Management

section.description: A space for you to provide any other information you think is relevant.
section.title: Other issues

field.textValue: Not all formats will be open. Some raw instrument-generated files and working spreadsheet files may initially be in proprietary formats, but open-format versions will be provided whenever feasible for publication.
The project is not expected to re-use previously published research datasets as primary input data.

The project datasets and their metadata will be made findable through deposition in trusted repositories that provide persistent identifiers and standard metadata. The primary repository will be Zenodo.

Yes, where appropriate. Controlled terms, standardized units, and repository metadata fields will be used to describe the datasets, methods, instruments, and biological experiments. Where no suitable domain-specific ontology is available or necessary, consistent project-level terminology will be applied.
The data and metadata will be deposited in an Open Access repository (e.g. Zenodo), where the dataset records and associated metadata will be publicly visible.

Where necessary, an embargo will be applied to datasets containing results that may support future scientific publications or potential intellectual property protection. Public access to such datasets will be temporarily restricted. The embargo period will be determined on a case-by-case basis and lifted by the responsible researcher in consultation with the host institution.
Metadata will be collected during the project in internal research records and dataset documentation files.

Detailed protocols, exact formulation parameters, and other information constituting proprietary know-how may be withheld from public re-use where necessary to protect intellectual property or future exploitation.

The project will generate unpublished data, including preliminary and supporting research data. Such data will be securely stored, documented, and evaluated for long-term value. Data of scientific relevance will be deposited with access conditions defined according to publication, confidentiality, and intellectual property requirements.

General methods and protocol information will be provided in publications and dataset documentation, and non-confidential protocols may be shared through the selected repository. Detailed procedures and optimization steps that could disclose confidential know-how or affect intellectual property protection may not be published openly.

The data will be created through scaffold fabrication, material characterization, degradation and release studies, and in vitro biological evaluation performed within the project. The datasets will include experimental measurements, instrument output files, microscopy images, assay results, and associated documentation generated during the research process.

The project will generate scaffold fabrication data, characterization data, degradation and release data, and in vitro biological evaluation data. These will be recorded in tabular and spreadsheet formats (CSV, XLSX), instrument-generated output files from analytical and characterization methods (TXT, CSV, PDF, and proprietary instrument formats), microscopy and other image files (TIF, PNG, JPG), and supporting documentation including protocols, reports, and statistical analyses (DOCX, PDF, TXT).
